how_it_works: The bridge (index.ts) is a VoicePlugin that listens on eos.turn_complete (concurrent handler, index.ts:101-104) and runs processTurn fire-and-forget. processTurn (143-248) supersedes any in-flight generation by aborting the shared this.abortController (150-151), then streams TextStreamParts from streamResponse (250-275) which prepends this.history to the user message. text-delta parts are pushed as llm.delta (167-172); on a clean 'stop' finish it pushes llm.done and calls rememberTurn (213-219) which appends user+assistant to history and trims to maxHistoryTurns*2 (363-367). Barge-in arrives as interrupt.llm on Route.Critical (135-139) which is drained ahead of pending Route.Main tts.* packets (pipeline-bus.ts:276-288), so the spoken-prefix state reflects only what was applied before the interrupt. commitInterruptedHistory (335-361) rewrites the interrupted turn's assistant message to the spoken prefix: computeSpokenPrefix (315-323) filters word timestamps by w.endMs <= playedOutMs (cumulative-from-context-start clocks that share an origin) when both are present and playedOutMs>0, else falls back to the full accumulated spokenByContext text. contextId is per-turn (voice-agent-session.ts:678; turn-metrics deletes per contextId), and playout_progress is throttled to 200ms (playout-progress.ts:5,40), so playedOutMs lags low — under-counting heard words, the safe direction for the no-unheard-text invariant.

findings.title: Per-turn state maps leak unboundedly for errored and superseded turns
findings.evidence: processTurn sets turnUserText for EVERY turn at line 146 (`this.turnUserText.set(contextId, userText);`). On the error path it returns at 235 without rememberTurn/clearTurnState; on supersession the loop returns at 162/211/222 (`if (signal.aborted) return`) — also no cleanup. rememberTurn (303-308) only runs on clean completion, and trimHistory's reclaim loop only frees contexts that HAVE an assistant message in history: `for (const [ctx, msg] of this.assistantMsgByContext) { if (!this.history.includes(msg)) this.clearTurnState(ctx); }` (369-371). Errored/superseded turns never create an assistantMsgByContext entry, so trimHistory never visits them. Their turnUserText (and any spokenByContext/wordTimestampsByContext/playedOutMsByContext accumulated from tts.* packets) are orphaned and never deleted until close().
findings.recommendation: On every processTurn exit that does not call rememberTurn (error return at 235, and the signal.aborted returns at 162/211/222), call this.clearTurnState(contextId) — or wrap the attempt loop in try/finally that clears state for contexts which did not get committed. At minimum delete turnUserText for superseded/errored contexts so it cannot grow per-turn across a long call.
findings.checklist_ref: missing cleanup / unbounded growth (PRODUCTION-CHECKLIST §barge-in + reliability hardening G-series)

findings.title: interrupt.llm aborts whichever generation is current, ignoring the interrupt's contextId
findings.evidence: The interrupt handler unconditionally aborts the shared controller regardless of which turn the interrupt targets: `bus.on("interrupt.llm", (pkt) => { this.abortController?.abort(); this.abortController = null; this.commitInterruptedHistory((pkt as {contextId:string}).contextId); });`. this.abortController is a single shared field reassigned per turn (151). If an interrupt for an older speaking context (interrupt.llm carries the interrupted/assistant contextId — voice-agent-session.ts:952) is drained while a NEWER turn's generation is in flight, it aborts the new turn's controller, killing live generation for a turn the user did not interrupt. The history rewrite still uses the correct contextId, so history stays correct; only the abort target can be wrong.
findings.recommendation: Key the abort by contextId: store the in-flight controller per contextId (or capture the contextId the current controller belongs to) and only abort when pkt.contextId matches the generating turn. The contextId is already available in the packet — use it for the abort, not just the history commit.

findings.title: Early barge-in (<200ms of playout) over-records history via the spokenByContext fallback
findings.evidence: computeSpokenPrefix only takes the word-timestamp path when `playedOutMs !== undefined && playedOutMs > 0` (318), otherwise returns the FULL accumulated text sent to TTS: `return (this.spokenByContext.get(contextId) ?? "").trim();` (322). PlayoutProgressEmitter throttles progress to `PROGRESS_THROTTLE_MS = 200` and only emits once `total - lastEmitted >= 200` (playout-progress.ts:40). So for a barge-in within the first ~200ms of playout, no tts.playout_progress has fired yet, playedOutMs is undefined, and the bridge records the ENTIRE text already streamed to TTS (which streams faster than realtime) as 'spoken' — words the user never heard enter history. This is the documented fallback caveat, but it is a real over-attribution window on the paced (telephony/browser) path, not only headless.
findings.recommendation: Treat playedOutMs===0/undefined while word timestamps exist as '≈nothing heard' rather than 'fall back to full text': when word timestamps are present but no playout clock has advanced, prefer an empty/near-empty prefix (or use tts.playout_started + elapsed wall-clock) instead of the full TTS-sent text. Alternatively lower the first-progress emit so the clock is available sooner.
findings.checklist_ref: PRODUCTION-CHECKLIST.md:216 (record spoken prefix, not generated; LLM must not believe the user heard words that never played)

findings.title: Mid-generation interrupt with no spoken text appends a dangling user message
findings.evidence: In commitInterruptedHistory's not-yet-committed branch, when spoken is empty: `const userText = this.turnUserText.get(contextId); if (userText !== undefined) { this.history.push({role:"user", content:userText}); if (spoken) this.history.push({role:"assistant", content:spoken}); ... }`. With spoken==='' the assistant push is skipped, leaving history ending on a lone user message. The next turn's streamResponse builds `[...this.history, {role:"user", content:userText}]` (251), producing two consecutive user messages. The AI SDK Google provider merges same-role messages so it won't hard-error, but the model sees two user utterances with no assistant turn between — a degenerate transcript for the interrupted exchange.
findings.recommendation: Either skip pushing the user message entirely when there is nothing spoken (the exchange produced no heard reply), or push an explicit empty/'[interrupted]' assistant turn so role alternation is preserved. Match the committed-branch behavior, which removes the assistant rather than leaving a dangling user.

findings.title: Spoken-prefix word reconstruction joins with a single space, diverging from generated text
findings.evidence: `const heard = words.filter((w) => w.endMs <= playedOutMs); return heard.map((w) => w.word).join(" ");`. The reconstructed assistant history is built by space-joining word tokens, which will not faithfully reproduce the original spacing/punctuation of the generated text (e.g. contractions, punctuation-adjacent tokens, or providers that emit leading-whitespace-bearing word fields). History text drifts slightly from what was actually spoken.
findings.recommendation: If the TTS word timestamps preserve original substrings/offsets, slice the original generated text up to the last heard word's character boundary instead of space-joining tokens; otherwise document that history text is a token-join approximation.

findings.title: No tests for state-leak cleanup, contextId-scoped abort, or dangling-user case
findings.evidence: The 8 tests (all green) thoroughly cover the spoken-prefix ladder (word-ts+playout, ts-only fallback, no-playout fallback, mid-gen record, full-commit truncate) but there is no test that (a) an errored or superseded turn clears turnUserText/spokenByContext (the leak in finding 1), (b) an interrupt for an old context does not abort a newer in-flight turn (finding 2), or (c) a mid-gen interrupt with empty spoken does not corrupt next-turn role alternation (finding 4). These are exactly the interruption/concurrency edges the domain must hold.
findings.recommendation: Add regression tests: fire N error/superseded turns and assert the internal maps stay bounded (or expose size for assertion); interrupt context A while context B streams and assert B still emits llm.done; mid-gen interrupt before any tts.text and assert the next captured messages array has valid role alternation.
findings.checklist_ref: test quality (no coverage of interruption/concurrency cleanup edges)

summary: The spoken-prefix core is genuinely solid and the hard invariant holds: contextId is per-turn so word-timestamp and playout clocks share an origin (filter w.endMs<=playedOutMs is sound), Critical-route interrupts drain ahead of pending Main tts.* packets so truncation can never include text that arrived after the barge-in, and the 200ms playout throttle makes playedOutMs lag LOW (under-counting heard words — the safe direction). The precision ladder (word-ts -> tts.text fallback), commit-in-place vs mid-gen record, retry/abort/finish-reason validation, and stream idle-timeout with listener/timeout cleanup are all well built and well tested (8/8 green). The real issues are around the EDGES of that core: (1) HIGH — per-turn state maps (turnUserText etc.) leak unboundedly for errored/superseded turns because trimHistory only reclaims contexts that produced a committed assistant message; (2) MEDIUM — interrupt.llm aborts the current controller ignoring its own contextId, so a stale interrupt can kill a newer turn's live generation; (3) MEDIUM — a barge-in in the first ~200ms of playout (before the first throttled progress) falls back to the FULL text sent to TTS, over-recording unheard words into history on the paced path; plus low-severity dangling-user-message and token-join fidelity gaps. None ship-broken, but findings 1-3 are reliability/correctness holes worth fixing before calling this domain done.
